characters.physical_details: Average height for his age, slightly soft build, brown hair that he does not think about or style, unremarkable face that adults struggle to distinguish from other students. He wears his robes slightly untucked because he never remembers to straighten them. No scars or distinguishing marks at story start. After the Cruciatus in Chapter 6, he develops a faint tremor in his left hand that comes and goes. Nosebleeds become frequent from Chapter 11 onward when he practices the First Art.

key_relationships.miren_gale: Best friend since first year, Hufflepuff housemate. Their friendship is deep, easy, and unromantic. It fractures under the pressure of incompatible priorities across the novel.
key_relationships.duncan_flett: Housemate and friend, less close than Miren but part of the same daily orbit. Gavin's failure to protect Duncan is the novel's primary moral weight.
key_relationships.professor_babbling: Teacher who helps him once, then withdraws. Gavin respects her expertise and does not blame her for her fear.
key_relationships.amycus_carrow: Antagonist in the institutional sense. Carrow does not single Gavin out — Gavin is just one of many students Carrow brutalizes.

characters.occupation_status: Fifth-year Hufflepuff student. Mediocre grades across all subjects. Takes Ancient Runes as an elective. Failing most OWL subjects by spring term because all his time goes to the manuscript.
characters.literacy_languages: English only. Learns to read the First Art notation system across the novel, but this is not a natural language — it is a cognitive framework that restructures how the reader processes information.

state_transitions.change: First physical cost: bruised and cut from Carrow detention
Cruciatus Curse exposure. Develops intermittent tremor in left hand.
First successful First Art practice. Begins getting nosebleeds from substrate work.
Friendship with Miren breaks. Social isolation nearly complete.
Can perceive the magical substrate clearly. His perception is now fundamentally different from any other character's.
Demonstrates ability to edit the substrate reflexively. Makes the conscious choice not to intervene in the Battle of Hogwarts.

characters.availability: Present in every chapter. POV character throughout.

characters.physical_details: Tall for her age, dark hair worn in a practical plait, quick-moving, physically confident. Mixed heritage — her mother is Black British and Muggle, her father is white and a wizard. She has her mother's bone structure and her father's restless energy. A bruise on her jaw from Chapter 12 onward that she does not explain.

key_relationships.gavin_tench: Best friend. Increasingly worried about him, then frustrated, then grieving the friendship.
key_relationships.duncan_flett: Fellow Hufflepuff she actively tries to protect. She sees helping Duncan as a moral imperative, which sharpens her frustration with Gavin's detachment.
key_relationships.da_resistance: Connected to the DA network through Hufflepuff upperclassmen. She smuggles healing supplies and passes messages. She is not a leader but a reliable operative.

characters.occupation_status: Fifth-year Hufflepuff student. Better at practical magic than Gavin. Solid but not exceptional student. Takes Care of Magical Creatures and Muggle Studies (the latter now a propaganda course under Alecto Carrow).
characters.literacy_languages: English. Grew up bilingual — her mother speaks Yoruba at home — but Miren's Yoruba is passive. She understands it but responds in English.

state_transitions.change: Argument with Gavin marks the first real fracture in their friendship.
Deepens DA involvement. Takes physical risks. Acquires the bruise on her jaw from an incident she will not discuss.
Final confrontation with Gavin. Friendship breaks over incompatible priorities.
Finds Gavin during the Battle. Sees what he has become. The break is confirmed as permanent in its old form.
Visits Gavin after the battle. The relationship has changed irrevocably — she believes him now but cannot follow him.

characters.availability: Present in most chapters. Less present in Chapters 9, 13, and 15 where Gavin is most isolated. Returns with force in Chapters 14, 17, and 18.

characters.physical_details: Thin, fair-haired, light-skinned, moves carefully in spaces he used to move freely in. He has grown visibly thinner across the year. By winter he looks older than fifteen. After Chapter 12, he has a split lip that heals badly and a wariness in his posture that was not there before.

key_relationships.gavin_tench: Housemate. Duncan does not expect Gavin to save him — he is past expecting anyone to save him. Their interactions are quiet and tinged with the awareness that Gavin could do more and does not.
key_relationships.miren_gale: Miren actively tries to protect him. Duncan accepts her help with the complicated gratitude of someone who knows being helped also makes him more visible.

characters.occupation_status: Fifth-year Hufflepuff student, Muggle-born. His parents are both Muggle. He was a good student before this year. Now he is focused on survival, not grades. The Carrows have him on an informal list of students to watch.
characters.literacy_languages: English. He grew up in a Muggle household in Sheffield. His Muggle cultural knowledge is deep but he has learned to suppress references to it.

state_transitions.change: Visible bruises from Carrow targeting. The persecution becomes specific.
His situation visibly worsens. Weight loss, withdrawal from common room life.
Tortured with the Cruciatus Curse in front of the class. The novel's most brutal moment of regime violence.
Survives the Battle of Hogwarts. Present in the aftermath.

characters.availability: Present in the common room and class scenes throughout but increasingly withdrawn. Most visible in Chapters 4, 9, 12, and 18. Absent from several mid-novel chapters where Gavin is absorbed in work.

characters.physical_details: Squat, lopsided face, bad teeth, physically powerful in a graceless way. He sweats when he is nervous, which is more often than students realize. He wears Death Eater robes with the sleeves pushed up, as if classroom cruelty is manual labor.

key_relationships.alecto_carrow: Sister. They coordinate punishment strategies. He defers to her in private though he postures in public.
key_relationships.severus_snape: Nominally under Snape's authority. Resents Snape's control. Performs harder when he thinks Snape is watching.

characters.occupation_status: Dark Arts professor (mandatory). Death Eater. In charge of student discipline.
characters.literacy_languages: English. Barely literate by academic standards — he is not a scholar, he is an enforcer.

state_transitions.change: DA revolt overwhelms his authority. He loses control of the student body.
Participates in the Battle of Hogwarts on the Death Eater side. His fate is consistent with canon.

characters.availability: Present in detention, classroom, and corridor scenes. Most prominent in Chapters 2, 6, 12, and 16. Background menace throughout.

characters.physical_details: Middle-aged, thin, wears reading glasses on a chain. Her hands are ink-stained in a way that suggests decades of manuscript work. She dresses conservatively even by Hogwarts staff standards.

key_relationships.gavin_tench: Student in her Ancient Runes class. She recognizes the notation he brings her as genuinely unprecedented. She helps him once and then withdraws to protect herself.

characters.occupation_status: Professor of Ancient Runes at Hogwarts. She has been on staff for over twenty years. She is not a member of the Order of the Phoenix. She is a scholar who keeps her head down.
characters.literacy_languages: English, Latin, Elder Futhark, Ogham, and several other runic systems. The First Art notation is beyond her expertise, but she provides the key framework that lets Gavin begin decoding it.

state_transitions.change: Helps Gavin identify the notation system and gives him a translational framework.
When Gavin comes back for more help, she refuses. She is too frightened of what might happen if the Carrows learn she is assisting unauthorized research.

characters.availability: Present in Chapters 4 and 8. Background mention in class scenes elsewhere. She survives the Battle.

timeline.story_start: September 1, 1997 — start of the Hogwarts school year under Snape's headmastership and the Carrow regime
timeline.estimated_span: Nine months, September 1997 through early May 1998

seasonal_track.season: Autumn (September-November 1997). Shortening days, rain, early frost on the grounds. The castle is cold because the Carrows have reduced heating for the lower houses.
Late autumn to early winter (November-December 1997). Dark by mid-afternoon. Snow on the mountains around the castle. The lake freezes partially.
Deep winter (December 1997-January 1998). Christmas. Skeleton population at Hogwarts. Heavy snow. The common room fire is the warmest place.
Late winter to early spring (January-March 1998). Slow thaw. Mud on the grounds. Days lengthening but still cold. The Forbidden Forest begins to stir.
Spring (April-early May 1998). Warming. The grounds green. Flowers in the courtyards. The contrast between natural renewal and the regime's intensification.
Early May 1998. Spring night of the Battle (May 1-2). Cool night, clear sky. Dawn after the battle.

key_events.event: Start of term under Carrow regime
Gavin finds the footnote in A History of Magic
Gavin's first detention with Amycus Carrow
Gavin finds the manuscript in the sub-collection
Gavin decodes the First Art concept
Gavin translates the section on the Builders
Gavin translates the section on the failing wards
Gavin's first successful First Art practice
Gavin perceives the cosmic threat directly
Gavin realizes he could end the war

geography.primary_setting: Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry, Scottish Highlands

key_locations.name: Hufflepuff common room
key_locations.details: Below ground, near the kitchens. Round, earthy, warm-toned. Barrel entrance, low ceilings, plants in the windows that get only indirect light. The most comfortable common room but also the most enclosed — no dramatic views, no tower vantage points. Under the Carrow regime it becomes a refuge; students linger here longer than usual.

key_locations.name: Hogwarts library and Restricted Section
key_locations.details: The library is on an upper floor. The Restricted Section is roped off and supervised. Madam Pince is more paranoid than usual under the regime. The manuscript Gavin seeks is not here.

key_locations.name: The sub-collection
key_locations.details: A part of the castle that does not appear on standard maps or in the guide given to first-years. Accessible through a sequence of corridors on the seventh floor that do not always connect the same way. Not the Room of Requirement — older, less accommodating. A series of small stone rooms with shelves cut directly into the walls. The air is different here: drier, colder, and the stonework is older than the rest of the castle. The manuscript is stored on an unmarked shelf in the innermost room. This becomes Gavin's primary workspace.

key_locations.name: Dark Arts classroom
key_locations.details: Formerly Defense Against the Dark Arts. The room has been stripped of its defensive teaching materials. Amycus Carrow teaches here. The desks are pushed to the walls to make space for practical demonstrations. There are scorch marks and, by midyear, bloodstains that nobody cleans.

key_locations.name: Ancient Runes classroom
key_locations.details: Professor Babbling's classroom. Small, scholarly, lined with reference materials. One of the few rooms in the castle that still feels like a school this year.

key_locations.name: Great Hall
key_locations.details: Meals and assemblies. The house tables, the enchanted ceiling, the staff table where Snape sits in Dumbledore's chair. The atmosphere at meals is subdued. Students eat quickly and leave.

key_locations.name: Astronomy Tower
key_locations.details: The highest accessible point of the castle. Gavin goes here when he needs physical distance from the sub-collection. It is where he has his most direct perception of the wards and the thing beyond them.

key_locations.name: Detention chambers
key_locations.details: Various rooms repurposed by the Carrows for punishment. The specific rooms change — sometimes an empty classroom, sometimes the dungeon corridor. The inconsistency is part of the intimidation.

distances: Hufflepuff common room to the library: several flights of stairs, approximately five minutes
Library to the sub-collection entrance: through a seventh-floor corridor sequence that takes variable time depending on the castle's configuration — sometimes ten minutes, sometimes thirty
Hufflepuff common room to the Great Hall: two minutes, same floor level
Hufflepuff common room to the Dark Arts classroom: three floors up, approximately seven minutes
Great Hall to the Astronomy Tower: the longest internal walk in the castle, approximately fifteen minutes at normal pace

world_rules: The First Art is not castable with a wand. It operates below the level of wand magic — adjusting the rules that make wand magic possible rather than producing magical effects directly.

The First Art cannot be taught through explanation. The manuscript is a cognitive restructuring tool: prolonged engagement with it gradually changes how the reader's mind processes magical information. This is why Gavin cannot simply tell someone what he has learned.

The manuscript's notation system is not a language in the conventional sense. It is a representational framework designed by a non-human intelligence for a non-human mode of cognition. Gavin translates it imperfectly through the runic-cipher framework Babbling provides, but meaning is always approximate.

The Builders (the ancient civilization) operated magic as a substrate of their existence, not as a tool. They did not die; they relocated to somewhere the manuscript cannot adequately describe in terms translatable to human cognition.

The planetary wards are passive structures that make Earth undetectable to the entity beyond them. They are degrading over centuries. No one in the wizarding world is aware of them because perceiving them requires First Art cognition.

The entity beyond the wards is not sentient in any way that maps to human or wizarding understanding of sentience. It does not want, plan, or target. It simply occupies space, and its occupation of space is incompatible with the continued existence of magical life as wizards know it. The analogy is environmental, not predatory.
Practicing the First Art has physical costs: nosebleeds, visual distortion, headaches. These are consequences of a human brain doing something it was not designed for, not magical side effects.

The Carrow regime at Hogwarts operates through institutional cruelty: mandatory Dark Arts practice including Unforgivable Curses, blood-status persecution, and detention as torture. This is consistent with established canon.
Gavin is a fifth-year taking OWL examinations. His academic performance declines across the year. OWL exams would normally occur in June but are disrupted by the Battle.

The Battle of Hogwarts occurs May 1-2, 1998. Harry Potter, Voldemort, and all canon characters are present but exist at the periphery of this novel's perspective. Gavin has no significant interaction with canon protagonists.

power_structure.mechanism: Military force, Ministry control, and the fear that Voldemort's name carries. The regime is totalitarian in aspiration but depends on enforcers like the Carrows for local implementation.
Headmaster authority, backed by Death Eater appointment. Snape's actual loyalty is ambiguous to all characters in this novel. He is a distant, feared authority figure to Gavin.
Delegated authority from the regime, enforced through detention-as-torture and classroom brutality. Their power is real but dependent on the regime's backing — they are middle managers of cruelty.
Secret communication, mutual aid, symbolic defiance. The DA cannot contest the Carrows' physical power but can contest their psychological control.
Cognitive restructuring through prolonged engagement. This is not coercion — Gavin chooses to engage — but the transformation is not fully reversible.

objects.name: A History of Magic (textbook)
objects.origin: Standard Hogwarts textbook by Bathilda Bagshot
objects.status: The footnote on page 742 references the pre-wand magical substrate and cites a manuscript in the Hogwarts collection. The footnote is easy to miss — a parenthetical in small type about sources Bagshot consulted and rejected.

objects.name: The manuscript
objects.origin: Created by the Builders as a teaching tool. It was brought to Hogwarts at some unknown point in the castle's early history and stored in a part of the collection that predates the modern library system.
objects.status: Sheets of material that is not parchment — thin, flexible, slightly warm to the touch, and undamaged despite apparent age. The notation shifts and moves but not in the way wizarding portraits move — the movement is representational, showing relationships between concepts rather than depicting scenes. Gavin removes it from the sub-collection and keeps it hidden in his trunk.

objects.name: Gavin's wand
objects.origin: Purchased at Ollivanders before first year
objects.status: Unremarkable wand. As Gavin develops First Art ability, the wand becomes increasingly irrelevant to his practice. He still carries it because not carrying a wand would draw attention.

objects.name: Babbling's copy of Wexworth
objects.origin: A reference text on pre-Futhark notation systems
objects.status: Provides the translational framework Gavin needs. He returns it to Babbling's desk as instructed. He has memorized what he needs from it.

knowledge_state.knows: The First Art exists — a meta-magical substrate beneath all wand magic
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone. He tries to tell Miren in Chapter 6 and Babbling in Chapter 8 but neither understands or believes the full scope.

knowledge_state.knows: The Builders existed, created the First Art, and departed Earth rather than dying
Planetary wards exist and are failing, and something beyond them is incompatible with magical life
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone. He tries to tell Miren in Chapter 8. She does not believe him.

knowledge_state.knows: He can practice the First Art — editing the substrate directly
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Miren sees the quill demonstration in Chapter 11 but interprets it as a Disillusionment Charm. Nobody understands what he is actually doing.

knowledge_state.knows: He could end the Voldemort war by rewriting the grammar that makes lethal magic possible
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Everyone. This knowledge is the basis of his refusal to act in Chapter 17.

knowledge_state.knows: Gavin is obsessed with a manuscript and withdrawing from normal life
knowledge_state.hidden_from: Nobody — she is open about her concern. But she does not understand what the manuscript contains.

knowledge_state.knows: Gavin claims the wards around Earth are failing and something cosmic threatens all magical life
knowledge_state.hidden_from: She tells no one because she does not believe it until Chapter 17-18.

knowledge_state.knows: The notation system Gavin showed her is genuinely unprecedented and pre-dates all known runic systems
knowledge_state.hidden_from: She does not share this with other staff. She does not know the manuscript's full contents.

knowledge_state.knows: He is being targeted by the Carrows because of his blood status
knowledge_state.hidden_from: This is known to all students. What is hidden is how bad it has gotten — Duncan minimizes to protect himself and others.

environmental_constants: The Hufflepuff common room smells of earth, warm wood, and whatever the kitchens are preparing — the kitchens are directly adjacent and the scent seeps through.
Hogwarts under the Carrow regime is quieter than normal. Students move through corridors quickly and do not linger. The usual between-class chaos is subdued.
The sub-collection rooms have a dry, cold, mineral smell — like limestone caves. No torches; the light comes from the stones themselves, a faint luminescence that predates the castle's torch system.
The Astronomy Tower is always windy. On clear nights, the view of the Scottish Highlands is vast and indifferent.
The Forbidden Forest is audible from many parts of the grounds — birdsong during the day, unidentifiable sounds at night. The forest's edge is visible from the Hufflepuff common room windows.
Mealtimes in the Great Hall are punctuated by the sound of owls, the clink of institutional tableware, and the low hum of conversation that is less animated than in previous years.
The castle's temperature is irregular this year — colder in the lower floors where Hufflepuff and the dungeons are, warmer in the upper floors near the Carrows' quarters.
